data.title: The Cortex Caper
data.premise: You've got a simple cargo job on Persephone that's supposed to pay well and keep the Alliance off your back. Of course, nothing involving fancy Core World art collectors and a mysterious sealed crate is ever that simple.
data.central_question: Can you complete the delivery without getting the crew killed, arrested, or worse - unpaid?
data.healing_context: Medical supplies and basic field treatment can handle most injuries. Serious wounds require a proper doctor or medbay. The ship has basic medical facilities, but anything beyond patching up cuts and bruises needs professional help.
starting_inventory: a pistol with two spare clips
the cargo manifest for the sealed crate

setting.name: Persephone and the Cortex shipping lanes
setting.description: The border planet Persephone bustles with legitimate trade and less-than-legitimate opportunities. Its proximity to the Core makes it a hub for those who prefer to operate in the gray areas between Alliance law and frontier justice.
setting.tone_notes: Witty banter, moral ambiguity, and the constant tension between doing right and staying afloat. Think fast-talking cons, unexpected complications, and the kind of trouble that follows good intentions.

acts.title: Simple Job, Complex Problems
acts.description: You meet your employer and collect the cargo, but complications arise when you discover what you're really transporting and who else wants it.
acts.end_condition: The crew decides whether to complete the original job or pursue an alternative solution
locked_constraints: The true nature of the cargo cannot be fully revealed
No direct confrontation with Alliance forces
key_beats: Pick up the mysterious sealed crate
Discover the cargo is more valuable and dangerous than expected
Learn that others are pursuing the same prize

acts.title: Complications and Revelations
acts.description: The truth about the cargo comes out, rival parties make their moves, and you must navigate between competing interests while keeping your crew safe.
acts.end_condition: A confrontation forces you to choose between profit, principle, and survival
locked_constraints: Cannot permanently lose any crew members
Must maintain at least one viable path forward
key_beats: The cargo's true nature is revealed
A crew member's past connection to the job surfaces

acts.title: The Double Cross Double Cross
acts.description: With all cards on the table, you must outmaneuver your opponents, complete the job on your own terms, and get your crew paid and clear.
acts.end_condition: The job is resolved and the crew escapes Persephone with their freedom and dignity mostly intact
locked_constraints: Must provide a satisfying resolution to the central question
key_beats: Execute the plan to outmaneuver all parties
Reflect on the job's unexpected lessons

npcs.motivation: Keep Serenity flying and the crew together
npcs.secret: Has been secretly corresponding with the original owner of the stolen cargo

npcs.motivation: Complete his collection and maintain his reputation
npcs.secret: Is being blackmailed and needs this delivery to clear his debts

factions.name: Serenity crew
factions.description: Your loyal but sometimes questioning crew who depend on you for leadership and livelihood
factions.goals: Stay flying, stay free, and hopefully get paid

factions.name: Niska organization
factions.description: Ruthless criminal syndicate with a long reach and longer memory
factions.goals: Recover stolen property and maintain reputation for swift retribution

world_rules: The Alliance controls the Core worlds but has limited reach on the border planets
Reputation matters more than law in the criminal underworld
Loyalty can be bought, but trust must be earned
Every job has complications - the trick is surviving them

- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it

- Weave pending consequences into the narrative naturally when their triggers are near

- Do NOT include state_updates, health numbers, or mechanical data in your response
- Pace the story toward the current act's end condition. Check the CURRENT ACT PACING section for scene count and proximity
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.8, your passage should feel like it's approaching a climax or turning point — increase urgency and stakes

data.passagePreview: The morning sun casts long shadows across Persephone's dusty docking bays as you adjust your brown c

data.passage:
The morning sun casts long shadows across Persephone's dusty docking bays as you adjust your brown coat and check the time on your pocket watch. Another day, another job that's supposed to be simple. You've heard that song before, and it usually ends with someone shooting at you.

Kaylee emerges from Serenity's engine room, wiping grease from her hands with a satisfied grin. "She's purring like a kitten, Cap'n. Ready for whatever foolishness you've got planned this time." Her cheerful optimism never fails to lift your spirits, even when you're about to walk into what your gut tells you might be trouble.

Jayne leans against the cargo bay ramp, Vera cradled in his arms like a beloved pet. "So this art collector fella - he's payin' real money for us to haul one crate? Seems too easy. What's the catch?" His suspicious nature isn't always wrong, much as you hate to admit it.

The job came through legitimate channels - well, mostly legitimate. Atherton Wing, some fancy Core World type with more money than sense, needs a sealed crate transported discreetly from his estate to a private buyer on Boros. No questions asked, no inspections, just straight transport for five thousand credits. The kind of money that keeps Serenity flying for months.

Your comm unit chirps. Wing's expecting you at his estate in an hour to collect the cargo.

A.text: Head straight to Wing's estate to get this job started and the cargo secured
A.consequence: Direct approach, meets Wing as scheduled

B.text: Do some quick research on Atherton Wing before meeting him
B.consequence: Gains useful background information on Wing

C.text: Check with your contacts about any unusual activity around high-value cargo jobs
C.consequence: May uncover complications or draw unwanted attention

D.text: Gather the crew for a quick briefing about the job and potential complications
D.consequence: Crew coordination improves, potential issues discussed

Respond with this exact JSON structure:
{
  "state_updates": {
    "player_changes": {
      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],
      "status_effects": [
        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky", "consequence": "brief mechanical note" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

PASSAGE:
The morning sun casts long shadows across Persephone's dusty docking bays as you adjust your brown coat and check the time on your pocket watch. Another day, another job that's supposed to be simple. You've heard that song before, and it usually ends with someone shooting at you.

Kaylee emerges from Serenity's engine room, wiping grease from her hands with a satisfied grin. "She's purring like a kitten, Cap'n. Ready for whatever foolishness you've got planned this time." Her cheerful optimism never fails to lift your spirits, even when you're about to walk into what your gut tells you might be trouble.

Jayne leans against the cargo bay ramp, Vera cradled in his arms like a beloved pet. "So this art collector fella - he's payin' real money for us to haul one crate? Seems too easy. What's the catch?" His suspicious nature isn't always wrong, much as you hate to admit it.

The job came through legitimate channels - well, mostly legitimate. Atherton Wing, some fancy Core World type with more money than sense, needs a sealed crate transported discreetly from his estate to a private buyer on Boros. No questions asked, no inspections, just straight transport for five thousand credits. The kind of money that keeps Serenity flying for months.

Your comm unit chirps. Wing's expecting you at his estate in an hour to collect the cargo.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Head straight to Wing's estate to get this job started and the cargo secured
B: Do some quick research on Atherton Wing before meeting him
C: Check with your contacts about any unusual activity around high-value cargo jobs
D: Gather the crew for a quick briefing about the job and potential complications

data.passagePreview: You gather Kaylee and Jayne in the cargo bay, their faces expectant in the dim lighting. The sealed

data.passage:
You gather Kaylee and Jayne in the cargo bay, their faces expectant in the dim lighting. The sealed crate sits between you like an elephant in the room—unmarked, unremarkable, and somehow ominous for all its ordinariness.

"Alright, here's the situation," you begin, hands clasped behind your back in that way that means business. "Simple cargo run to the Cortex shipping lanes. Client's paying well—maybe too well for something this straightforward."

Kaylee's brow furrows as she studies the crate. "Cap'n, this thing's got some serious shielding. Whatever's inside, somebody doesn't want it scanned."

Jayne hefts Vera, checking her sight. "How serious we talking? 'Cause I got a feeling simple jobs don't come with this kind of coin attached."

"That's exactly what worries me," you admit. "Wing seemed nervous, kept checking his timepiece like he was expecting company. Could be nothing, could be we're walking into someone else's mess."

Kaylee shifts uncomfortably. "You think maybe we should—"

She's cut off by footsteps on the cargo ramp. Atherton Wing appears, impeccably dressed and visibly agitated, flanked by two nervous-looking assistants.

"Captain Reynolds! Thank goodness you're still here. We have a... situation."

A.text: Ask Wing directly what kind of trouble he's brought to your ship
A.consequence: Direct confrontation may reveal dangerous information but could strain relationship with Wing

B.text: Signal Jayne to keep his gun ready while you listen to Wing's explanation
B.consequence: Cautious preparation while gathering information

C.text: Demand to know what's really in the crate before you go anywhere
C.consequence: Demanding answers may force Wing to reveal more than he intended, potentially complicating the situation

D.text: Stay calm and professional—let Wing reveal his problem at his own pace
D.consequence: Professional approach maintains good standing while learning about the problem

- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Crew coordination improves, potential issues discussed"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You gather Kaylee and Jayne in the cargo bay, their faces expectant in the dim lighting. The sealed crate sits between you like an elephant in the room—unmarked, unremarkable, and somehow ominous for all its ordinariness.

"Alright, here's the situation," you begin, hands clasped behind your back in that way that means business. "Simple cargo run to the Cortex shipping lanes. Client's paying well—maybe too well for something this straightforward."

Kaylee's brow furrows as she studies the crate. "Cap'n, this thing's got some serious shielding. Whatever's inside, somebody doesn't want it scanned."

Jayne hefts Vera, checking her sight. "How serious we talking? 'Cause I got a feeling simple jobs don't come with this kind of coin attached."

"That's exactly what worries me," you admit. "Wing seemed nervous, kept checking his timepiece like he was expecting company. Could be nothing, could be we're walking into someone else's mess."

Kaylee shifts uncomfortably. "You think maybe we should—"

She's cut off by footsteps on the cargo ramp. Atherton Wing appears, impeccably dressed and visibly agitated, flanked by two nervous-looking assistants.

"Captain Reynolds! Thank goodness you're still here. We have a... situation."

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Ask Wing directly what kind of trouble he's brought to your ship
B: Signal Jayne to keep his gun ready while you listen to Wing's explanation
C: Demand to know what's really in the crate before you go anywhere
D: Stay calm and professional—let Wing reveal his problem at his own pace

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You lean back against the cargo crate, arms crossed, and give Wing the kind of patient look you've perfected over years of dealing with nervous clients. "Take your time, Mr. Wing. We're listening."

Kaylee stops her tinkering with the cargo straps and moves closer, her natural curiosity getting the better of her. Jayne just grunts and continues cleaning his gun, though you notice his ears are perked up.

Wing wrings his hands, glancing nervously at his two assistants—a thin man in expensive clothes and a woman who looks more like hired muscle than art appreciation. "The thing is, Captain Reynolds, it seems word has gotten out about our little transaction. My sources tell me that Adelai Niska has taken a... personal interest in this particular piece."

The name hits the air like a dropped wrench. Kaylee's eyes widen, and even Jayne looks up from his weapon.

"Now, I'm not saying he knows the specifics," Wing continues quickly, "but he's been asking questions. Uncomfortable questions. About art collectors, shipping manifests, and—" He swallows hard. "—about Firefly-class transports operating out of this dock."

You feel that familiar tightness in your gut that comes with jobs getting complicated. The kind of complicated that involves people with reputations for creative violence.

A.text: Ask Wing exactly what he knows about Niska's interest in the cargo
A.consequence: Gather intelligence about Niska's specific interest without revealing your concerns

B.text: Demand to know what Wing isn't telling you about this 'art piece'
B.consequence: Aggressive questioning might reveal important information but could damage trust with Wing

C.text: Suggest moving the cargo immediately before Niska gets closer
C.consequence: Hasty action might avoid immediate danger but could draw unwanted attention

D.text: Keep your poker face and ask what Wing proposes to do about it
D.consequence: Professional composure maintains control of the situation while learning Wing's plan

      "inventory": ["full current inventory list"],

        {
          "id": "string — unique identifier (e.g. broken_arm_001)",
          "name": "string — display name (e.g. Broken Arm)",
          "description": "string — current narrative description of the condition",
          "severity": "number 0.0-1.0 — how debilitating (1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.5 = significant, 0.1 = minor)",
          "time_remaining": "{ days, hours, minutes, seconds } OR null if no auto-expiry",
          "removal_condition": "string describing what removes this effect, OR null if timer-only",
          "lethal": "boolean — if true, character dies when timer expires unresolved"
        }
      ],
      "skills": ["full current skills list"]
    },
    "time_elapsed": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 },
    "new_pending_consequences": [ { "id": "string", "description": "string", "trigger": "string", "severity": "string", "time_remaining": { "days": 0, "hours": 0, "minutes": 0, "seconds": 0 } } ],
    "resolved_consequences": [ "ids of consequences that fired this turn" ],
    "event_log_entry": "string — one-line summary of what happened",
    "world_flag_changes": { "flag_name": true/false },
    "relationship_changes": { "npc_or_faction_name": number_delta },
    "npc_updates": { "npc_name": { "role": "string (optional)", "motivation": "string (optional)", "allegiance": "string (optional)", "secret_revealed": "boolean (optional)", "companion": "boolean (optional)", "notes": "string — brief freeform context (optional)" } },
    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",
    "proximity_to_climax": "number 0.0-1.0 — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",
    "game_over": false,
    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky", "consequence": "brief mechanical note" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.
Pre-classified outcome: ADVANCE_SAFE
Pre-determined consequence: "Professional approach maintains good standing while learning about the problem"

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You lean back against the cargo crate, arms crossed, and give Wing the kind of patient look you've perfected over years of dealing with nervous clients. "Take your time, Mr. Wing. We're listening."

Kaylee stops her tinkering with the cargo straps and moves closer, her natural curiosity getting the better of her. Jayne just grunts and continues cleaning his gun, though you notice his ears are perked up.

Wing wrings his hands, glancing nervously at his two assistants—a thin man in expensive clothes and a woman who looks more like hired muscle than art appreciation. "The thing is, Captain Reynolds, it seems word has gotten out about our little transaction. My sources tell me that Adelai Niska has taken a... personal interest in this particular piece."

The name hits the air like a dropped wrench. Kaylee's eyes widen, and even Jayne looks up from his weapon.

"Now, I'm not saying he knows the specifics," Wing continues quickly, "but he's been asking questions. Uncomfortable questions. About art collectors, shipping manifests, and—" He swallows hard. "—about Firefly-class transports operating out of this dock."

You feel that familiar tightness in your gut that comes with jobs getting complicated. The kind of complicated that involves people with reputations for creative violence.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Ask Wing exactly what he knows about Niska's interest in the cargo
B: Demand to know what Wing isn't telling you about this 'art piece'
C: Suggest moving the cargo immediately before Niska gets closer
D: Keep your poker face and ask what Wing proposes to do about it

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
"Right then," you say, clapping your hands together with the kind of false confidence that's gotten you out of more scrapes than you care to count. "We move the cargo now, before Niska's boys get any closer to sniffing us out." Wing's face goes pale at the suggestion, but you're already turning to your crew. "Kaylee, get Serenity prepped for immediate departure. Jayne, you're with me - we're moving that crate double-time."

Kaylee nods enthusiastically, already heading for the engine room. "I can have her ready in twenty minutes, Cap'n!" But Jayne crosses his arms, that familiar look of mercenary calculation creeping across his face.

"Now hold on there, Mal," he says slowly. "Moving cargo in broad daylight, all rushed-like? That's gonna draw attention. Port Authority, maybe even Alliance patrols. And if Niska's got eyes on this dock..." He trails off meaningfully.

Wing wrings his hands. "Perhaps we should reconsider the timing. My contacts suggested a more... discrete evening transfer."

You glance at the sealed crate sitting innocently in the cargo bay. Whatever's inside, it's valuable enough to make Niska interested and Wing desperate. The question is whether moving fast and loud is better than moving slow and careful - especially when you still don't know exactly what game you're playing.

A.text: Override their concerns and move the cargo immediately anyway
A.consequence: Immediate action draws Port Authority attention and strains crew loyalty

B.text: Wait until evening but use the time to scout for Niska's men
B.consequence: Cautious approach provides valuable intelligence but uses precious time

C.text: Demand Wing tell you exactly what's in the crate before you risk your crew
C.consequence: Confronting Wing might reveal crucial information but could damage the business relationship

D.text: Split the difference - move to a different, less obvious location on Persephone
D.consequence: Compromise solution reduces immediate risk while maintaining crew confidence

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

PASSAGE:
"Right then," you say, clapping your hands together with the kind of false confidence that's gotten you out of more scrapes than you care to count. "We move the cargo now, before Niska's boys get any closer to sniffing us out." Wing's face goes pale at the suggestion, but you're already turning to your crew. "Kaylee, get Serenity prepped for immediate departure. Jayne, you're with me - we're moving that crate double-time."

Kaylee nods enthusiastically, already heading for the engine room. "I can have her ready in twenty minutes, Cap'n!" But Jayne crosses his arms, that familiar look of mercenary calculation creeping across his face.

"Now hold on there, Mal," he says slowly. "Moving cargo in broad daylight, all rushed-like? That's gonna draw attention. Port Authority, maybe even Alliance patrols. And if Niska's got eyes on this dock..." He trails off meaningfully.

Wing wrings his hands. "Perhaps we should reconsider the timing. My contacts suggested a more... discrete evening transfer."

You glance at the sealed crate sitting innocently in the cargo bay. Whatever's inside, it's valuable enough to make Niska interested and Wing desperate. The question is whether moving fast and loud is better than moving slow and careful - especially when you still don't know exactly what game you're playing.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Override their concerns and move the cargo immediately anyway
B: Wait until evening but use the time to scout for Niska's men
C: Demand Wing tell you exactly what's in the crate before you risk your crew
D: Split the difference - move to a different, less obvious location on Persephone

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

data.passage:
You make the call with the kind of decisive authority that got you through the war. "We're movin' the cargo, but not off-world. Not yet." You turn to Kaylee, whose worried expression eases slightly. "Find us a secure warehouse in the industrial district - somewhere with multiple exits and no nosy neighbors." She nods, already pulling up dock records on her portable cortex unit.

Jayne hefts Vera with obvious relief. "Now you're talkin' sense, Mal. I was startin' to think you'd gone soft."

"Soft's got nothin' to do with it," you reply, checking your timepiece. "Smart's what keeps us breathin'. Wing, you got contacts who can arrange discrete storage?"

Wing wrings his hands nervously. "Perhaps... yes, I believe so. Though the additional expense..."

"Bill it to hazard pay," you cut him off as Kaylee waves you over to her screen.

"Cap'n, I found somethin' perfect. Old textile warehouse, been empty six months. Owner's offworld, and the security system's been disconnected to save credits." She grins. "Plus, it's got loading docks that connect to three different transit routes."

As you study the warehouse specs, your cortex unit chimes with an incoming wave. The sender ID makes your blood run cold - it's marked with Niska's personal encryption signature.

A.text: Answer Niska's wave immediately - better to know what he wants
A.consequence: Direct confrontation with Niska creates immediate pressure and reveals crew position

B.text: Ignore the wave and move the cargo to the warehouse right now
B.consequence: Quick action secures cargo but leaves Niska's intentions unknown

C.text: Have Wing answer the wave while you prepare for trouble
C.consequence: Using Wing as intermediary creates complications and potential betrayal

D.text: Send Kaylee and Jayne ahead while you deal with Niska's call
D.consequence: Smart delegation allows handling multiple priorities simultaneously

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

The Writer produced the following passage and choices this turn.

PASSAGE:
You make the call with the kind of decisive authority that got you through the war. "We're movin' the cargo, but not off-world. Not yet." You turn to Kaylee, whose worried expression eases slightly. "Find us a secure warehouse in the industrial district - somewhere with multiple exits and no nosy neighbors." She nods, already pulling up dock records on her portable cortex unit.

Jayne hefts Vera with obvious relief. "Now you're talkin' sense, Mal. I was startin' to think you'd gone soft."

"Soft's got nothin' to do with it," you reply, checking your timepiece. "Smart's what keeps us breathin'. Wing, you got contacts who can arrange discrete storage?"

Wing wrings his hands nervously. "Perhaps... yes, I believe so. Though the additional expense..."

"Bill it to hazard pay," you cut him off as Kaylee waves you over to her screen.

"Cap'n, I found somethin' perfect. Old textile warehouse, been empty six months. Owner's offworld, and the security system's been disconnected to save credits." She grins. "Plus, it's got loading docks that connect to three different transit routes."

As you study the warehouse specs, your cortex unit chimes with an incoming wave. The sender ID makes your blood run cold - it's marked with Niska's personal encryption signature.

CHOICES PRESENTED TO PLAYER:
A: Answer Niska's wave immediately - better to know what he wants
B: Ignore the wave and move the cargo to the warehouse right now
C: Have Wing answer the wave while you prepare for trouble
D: Send Kaylee and Jayne ahead while you deal with Niska's call

Based on the passage and the current game state, determine:
1. State updates — what changed mechanically as a result of this passage (status effects, inventory, time_elapsed)
2. Choice metadata — classify each choice and predetermine its outcome for the next turn

Respond with ONLY the JSON object.

data.passage:
You make the call quickly. "Kaylee, Jayne - get that crate to the warehouse. I'll handle our uninvited friend here." Kaylee nods, understanding immediately, while Jayne just grunts and hefts his end of the cargo with renewed purpose. They disappear into Persephone's maze of loading docks, leaving you alone with the blinking comm unit.

Niska's face fills the screen when you accept the wave - all predatory smile and cold calculation. "Captain Reynolds. Such a pleasure to finally speak with you directly." His accent makes even pleasantries sound like threats. "I believe you have something that belongs to me. A certain... artifact... that was taken from my collection under unfortunate circumstances."

Behind him, you catch glimpses of his enforcers - the kind of men who solve problems with violence first and questions later. "Now, I am reasonable man," Niska continues, "so I make you reasonable offer. Bring me my property, and I pay you double what Wing promised. Plus, you keep breathing. This is good deal, yes?"

The comm crackles with interference, and through the static, you hear the distant sound of Alliance patrol ships in the upper atmosphere. Your window for making this decision is closing fast, and whatever you choose will determine whether you're completing Wing's job, switching sides, or finding a third option entirely.

A.text: Accept Niska's deal - it's more money and fewer complications
A.consequence: Accepting Niska's deal creates complications with Wing and potential crew loyalty issues

B.text: Refuse outright and warn him to stay clear of your crew
B.consequence: Direct refusal escalates conflict with Niska while Alliance patrols complicate escape

C.text: Stall for time while you figure out what this 'artifact' really is
C.consequence: Buying time allows gathering crucial information about the artifact's true nature

D.text: Propose a counter-offer that might benefit everyone involved
D.consequence: Counter-proposal opens diplomatic solution that could satisfy multiple parties

    "game_over": false,

  },

    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky", "consequence": "brief mechanical note" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:
The player chose option D.

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:
- ADVANCE_SAFE: No new negative status effects. Existing effects may improve. No penalties.
- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

data.passagePreview: You lean back in your chair, letting a slow smile cross your face as Niska's image flickers on the s

data.passage:
You lean back in your chair, letting a slow smile cross your face as Niska's image flickers on the screen. "Now, Mr. Niska, seems to me we got ourselves a situation where everybody's grabbing for the same prize. But what if there was a way for all parties to walk away satisfied?"

Niska's eyes narrow with interest. "Continue, Captain Reynolds."

"Wing's got his reputation to maintain, you want what's rightfully yours, and I need to keep my crew fed. The cargo's valuable enough to split three ways and still make everyone happy." You pause for effect. "Course, that assumes Wing's willing to renegotiate his exclusive arrangement."

Kaylee shoots you an impressed look from where she's monitoring the cargo transfer, while Jayne just grunts his approval of any plan that doesn't involve immediate gunplay.

"Interesting proposition," Niska muses. "But how do I know this Wing will honor such arrangement? He has already proven... unreliable in past dealings."

Before you can respond, your comm crackles with an urgent transmission from Wash: "Mal, we got Alliance patrol ships making a sweep of the docking bays. They're about ten minutes out from our sector."

Niska's smile grows cold. "It appears time for negotiation grows short, Captain. Perhaps we should discuss this face to face. I am already en route to Persephone."

A.text: Arrange an immediate three-way meeting with Wing and Niska
A.consequence: High-stakes meeting with all parties present creates volatile situation

B.text: Stall Niska while rushing the cargo off-world before he arrives
B.consequence: Racing against time while Niska pursues creates chase scenario

C.text: Contact Wing first to get his agreement before meeting with Niska
C.consequence: Diplomatic preparation strengthens negotiating position

D.text: Use the Alliance patrol as leverage to force a quick resolution
D.consequence: Using external pressure forces quick but favorable resolution

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.

PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE:

Your state_updates MUST be consistent with this pre-classified outcome:

- ADVANCE_RISKY: Moderate consequences — new status effects or worsened existing ones are appropriate.
- SEVERE_PENALTY: Heavy consequences — serious injuries, dangerous status effects as described.
- DEATH: set game_over to true.
- HIDDEN_BENEFIT: apply the hidden benefit described in the consequence.

data.passage:
You key up Wing's frequency while Kaylee keeps watch and Jayne checks his weapons for what feels like the hundredth time. The art collector's face appears on the small screen, looking considerably more haggard than when this job started.

"Captain Reynolds! Please tell me you have good news. The Alliance patrol just docked three berths down from me, and they're asking very pointed questions about cargo manifests."

"Actually, Wing, I might have a solution that keeps everyone breathing and you out of Alliance custody." You explain Niska's proposal for a three-way split, watching Wing's expression cycle through disbelief, calculation, and what might be hope.

"You're suggesting I negotiate with Adelai Niska? The man who once fed someone to his dogs over a parking dispute?"

"I'm suggesting we all walk away from this with something instead of nothing," you reply. "Niska gets part of his property back, you get enough to clear your debts, and I get paid for services rendered. Alternative is we all explain to the Alliance why we're fighting over stolen goods."

Kaylee catches your eye and points toward the docking bay entrance. "Cap'n, those Alliance boys are moving this way, and they got that official look about them."

Wing's voice drops to a whisper. "If I agree to this madness, what guarantee do I have that Niska won't simply take everything and kill us all anyway?"

A.text: Promise to handle the negotiations personally and keep everyone honest
A.consequence: Personal guarantee creates obligation but builds trust with Wing

B.text: Suggest meeting in a public place where Niska can't pull anything violent
B.consequence: Public venue reduces violence risk and improves negotiation dynamics

C.text: Propose using the approaching Alliance patrol as unwitting protection
C.consequence: Using Alliance as shield is clever but increases their interest in the situation

D.text: Offer to split your own cut with Wing as insurance against betrayal
D.consequence: Financial gesture demonstrates good faith and secures Wing's cooperation

        }

    },

    "new_scene_context": "string — brief context for next passage",

    "advance_act": "true or false — REQUIRED (see PACING)",

    "story_complete": "true when Act 3 end_condition is met (see PACING)"
  },
  "choice_metadata": {
    "A": { "outcome": "advance_safe|advance_risky", "consequence": "brief mechanical note" },
    "B": { ... }, "C": { ... }, "D": { ... }
  }
}

RULES:
- Respond with ONLY the JSON object — nothing before it, nothing after it
- Only include player_changes, relationship_changes, world_flag_changes, new_pending_consequences, and resolved_consequences when they actually changed. Always include: time_elapsed, event_log_entry, proximity_to_climax, advance_act.
- Relationship changes are DELTAS, not absolute values
- proximity_to_climax: REQUIRED every turn — set this value in state_updates using the formula in the PACING section below
- event_log_entry is required — always summarize what happened this turn
- choice_metadata must classify all four choices (A, B, C, D)
- CRITICAL: When a PLAYER'S PREVIOUS CHOICE section is provided, your state_updates MUST honor the pre-classified outcome. If a choice was advance_safe, do NOT apply negative status effects or penalties. The pre-classified outcome is the single source of truth for mechanical impact.
- npc_updates: include when an NPC's role, motivation, allegiance, or companion status changes, or when a narratively significant new NPC is introduced that the player will interact with again. For existing skeleton NPCs, use their exact name. For new NPCs, use the character's name as the key and include at least role and motivation. Do not create entries for one-off background characters (shopkeepers, random guards, etc.).

PACING — REQUIRED:
- The current act's target_scenes (in the skeleton) is the intended scene count for this act.
- Compute scenes elapsed in this act: scene_number - act_start_scene + 1 (both values are in CURRENT POSITION above).
- Set proximity_to_climax = (scenes_in_act / target_scenes), clamped to [0.0, 1.0]. Include this in state_updates EVERY turn.
- MINIMUM SCENES: Do NOT set advance_act to true if scenes_in_act < 3. Every act needs at least 3 scenes to develop properly.
- If scenes_in_act >= target_scenes AND the act's end_condition is narratively close to being met, set advance_act to true.
- If scenes_in_act exceeds target_scenes by 3 or more, you SHOULD set advance_act to true — the act has gone on too long. Drive the narrative forward.
- When advance_act is true, also set proximity_to_climax to 1.0.
- STORY COMPLETION: If the current act is Act 3 (the FINAL act) and its end_condition is met, set story_complete to true INSTEAD of advance_act. This ends the story.
- When proximity_to_climax >= 0.7, your choice_metadata should steer toward the act's end_condition — offer choices that could trigger it.

TIME ELAPSED — REQUIRED EVERY TURN:
- You MUST include time_elapsed in state_updates to indicate how much in-game time passed during this scene.
- Estimate realistically: a brief conversation = 5-15 minutes, a fight = 1-5 minutes, traveling across a city = 30-60 minutes, a journey = hours or days, sleeping/resting = 6-8 hours.
- The client uses time_elapsed to advance the in-game clock and tick down status effect timers.

STATUS EFFECTS — CRITICAL:
- Status effects are the PRIMARY way to track injuries, conditions, and afflictions. Do NOT use health_delta.
- status_effects is the FULL current list every turn (replace semantics). Include ALL active effects, adjusting severity and descriptions as appropriate.
- When the character is injured, add a status effect instead of reducing health. Example: a stab wound becomes { id: "stab_wound_001", name: "Stab Wound", description: "A deep cut in your side that bleeds freely", severity: 0.8, time_remaining: { days: 5, hours: 0, minutes: 0, seconds: 0 }, lethal: false }.
- Adjust severity each turn based on context: resting lowers severity, aggravation raises it, medicine/magic accelerates healing.
- Severity scale: 1.0 = completely debilitating, 0.7 = severely impaired, 0.5 = significant but manageable, 0.3 = noticeable nuisance, 0.1 = almost healed.
- time_remaining can be null for effects with no natural expiry (e.g. a curse that requires a specific action to remove).
- removal_condition can be set for effects that need specific actions (e.g. "Find the Witch of the Moor to break this curse"). Can be combined with time_remaining.
- Healing times should be genre/setting-appropriate. Consult the HEALING CONTEXT above. Realistic settings = realistic healing (broken bone: weeks, minor cut: days). Fantasy/sci-fi = faster if the setting provides healing methods.

PENDING CONSEQUENCES:
- New consequences use time_remaining ({ days, hours, minutes, seconds }) instead of scenes_remaining. Estimate how much in-game time before the consequence triggers.
- The client ticks down consequence time_remaining by time_elapsed each turn.

NORMAL MODE RULES (MANDATORY):
- NEVER set game_over to true. The player cannot die on Normal.
- NEVER create lethal status effects.
- Maximum status effect severity: 0.8. Injuries matter but are never fatal.
- Healing is realistic for the setting. A serious wound takes days to heal, but the player should have opportunities to find medicine or rest.
- Consequences are meaningful but recoverable: injuries, lost items, relationship damage
- Approximately 2 safe and 2 risky choices per turn.
- NPCs can be upset but always have a path to reconciliation.
